q: The pitch of the note is determined by its frequency.
The greater the frequency, the higher is the pitch.
Higher frequency results in a lower pitch of the note.
The loudness of the note depends upon the amplitude of the wave that produces it.
The greater the amplitude, the louder the note.
The greater the amplitude, the softer the note becomes.
Less energy is used to produce a larger amplitude.
More energy is used to produce a larger amplitude.
Quality of a sound is a characteristic that distinguishes sounds of different loudness.
Quality of a sound distinguishes two sounds of the same loudness and same pitch.
Timbre is another name for the quality of a sound.
Notes of the same pitch and loudness can be distinguished by their quality.
The timbre of a sound depends on its frequency.
The timbre of a sound depends on its waveform.
Waveform varies with the number of overtones or harmonics.
The notes produced by the violin are richer because it has fewer overtones.
Violin notes are richer because they have more overtones.
The intensity of the sound at a place is the energy transmitted per second per unit area.
The intensity of sound is the energy transmitted per minute per unit area.
Unit area for intensity measurement is held normally to the direction of sound.
The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the amplitude of the wave.
The intensity of a wave is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude of the wave.
The relationship $I \propto A^2$ is correct for intensity and amplitude.
Loudness is a physical property that does not depend on the observer.
Loudness is a sensation and depends on the observer.
To the human ears, the loudness of the sound is objective.
To the human ears, the loudness of the sound is subjective.
Normally the greater the intensity, the greater the loudness of the sound is.
Low intensity sounds are always perceived as louder than high intensity sounds.
Angular frequency is a factor in the expression for intensity of sound.
Density of the medium is represented by $\rho$ in the intensity formula.
The variable $v$ in the intensity formula represents the velocity of the sound wave.
The intensity of a wave is inversely proportional to the square of the amplitude.
Two different musical instruments can produce notes of the same pitch.
Harmonics do not affect the waveform of a sound.
The intensity level is identical to the sensation of loudness.
Some people may find a volume of sound loud while others find it soft.
The timbre of a sound depends on the number of harmonics.
The energy transmitted per second per unit area defines the loudness.
Frequency determines how high a note is.
Frequency determines the amplitude of the wave.
Pitch and loudness are the same property of sound.
A larger amplitude is produced when more energy is used.
The quality of a sound distinguishes notes produced by different instruments.
The intensity formula $I = \frac{1}{2} \rho v \omega^2 A^2$ is correct.
Sound intensity is the energy transmitted per unit volume.
Intensity is proportional to the square of the angular frequency.
Timbre depends on the observer's subjective hearing.
Intensity is a sensation.
Frequency is the only factor determining the loudness of a sound.
